dimensions.name: Past performance relevance
dimensions.reasoning: Near-perfect past performance alignment. Maryland DHS Benefits Analytics Platform (similar proposal #1, $4.2M, state benefits program) provides direct state-level benefits administration experience. Commerce Trade Data Modernization (similar proposal #2, $8.4M) demonstrates federal civilian Snowflake/dbt/Airflow delivery at comparable scale. CMS Healthcare Data Pipeline Modernization (similar proposal #3, $6.1M) shows federal benefits program experience with pipeline migration. RFP Section 4 requires 'three past performance citations from the last five years, at least two federal civilian, minimum $2.5M each, preference for federal benefits programs and legacy ETL modernization' - we can provide all three with direct relevance. Only gap preventing a 95+ score is that none of our past work was specifically SNAP/FNS, though Maryland DHS and CMS are closely adjacent.

dimensions.name: Technical capability match
dimensions.reasoning: Exceptional technical alignment. RFP Section 2 and 3 requirements map directly to our core capabilities: (1) Snowflake platform on AWS GovCloud - company profile lists 'Snowflake and Databricks delivery experience across 40+ projects' as core capability; (2) dbt and Apache Airflow - profile states 'Production data pipelines using Airflow, dbt, Fivetran'; (3) SSIS migration - profile explicitly mentions 'Strong track record migrating SSIS and Informatica workloads to modern orchestration' and RFP requires 'minimum 3 completed migrations' which we can demonstrate; (4) Tableau dashboards with row-level security - profile lists 'Tableau, Power BI, and Looker dashboard development' as core; (5) Data quality framework - profile lists 'custom data quality frameworks' as core capability; (6) FISMA Moderate - profile shows ISO 27001:2022 certification and federal delivery experience. The only requirement not explicitly in our core list is the specific '19 SSIS pipelines' scale, but similar proposal #3 mentions '17 legacy Informatica pipelines' migration, demonstrating comparable scope.

dimensions.name: Geographic and clearance alignment
dimensions.reasoning: Good but not perfect alignment. RFP Section 1 specifies 'Place of Performance: Alexandria, VA (hybrid; 30% on-site)' which is in our primary DMV geography per company profile. Company headquarters in Silver Spring, MD provides easy access to Alexandria, VA location. However, 30% on-site requirement (roughly 1.5 days/week) is more than our typical 'remote delivery supported for 90% of work' model, requiring dedicated local staff assignment. RFP Section 3 requires 'Active Facility Clearance at Secret level minimum' which we hold per company profile. We have 14 staff with clearances including 3 TS/SCI, sufficient to staff Secret-level work. Score is 82 rather than 90+ due to the on-site requirement being slightly higher than our typical engagement model, though still manageable given DMV location.

rationale.confidence_notes: RFP is detailed and unambiguous. Technical requirements map directly to our core capabilities. Past performance requirements align precisely with our portfolio. No significant unknowns that would change the assessment.
rationale.effort_reasoning: 30-page technical volume with 61-day timeline is standard effort. High reuse potential from three similar past proposals (benefits platform, federal data modernization, pipeline migration) reduces original content requirements. However, RFP requires detailed technical approach for 19-state SSIS pipeline migration with state-specific characteristics, anomaly detection framework design, and SNAP domain knowledge - all requiring original analysis and writing. Evaluation emphasizes technical approach and past performance, requiring strong narrative development. Overall: medium effort, likely 200-250 person-hours across capture, technical writing, and proposal management.
rationale.calibration_notes: Rubric performed well for this evaluation. The weighted dimensions captured all relevant decision factors. One potential rubric enhancement: consider adding a sub-dimension under 'technical_capability_match' that specifically addresses scale/complexity of prior work (e.g., number of pipelines migrated, number of data sources integrated) since RFPs often specify minimum thresholds like '3 completed SSIS migrations.' This would make the scoring more granular. Also note that the 'small business size standard' issue is a critical verification item that sits between hard disqualifier and deal-breaker - current rubric structure handled it appropriately by not triggering hard disqualifier (since we historically qualify) but flagging as high-severity deal-breaker requiring immediate verification.
rationale.recommendation_rationale: Exceptional alignment across all dimensions. This is a near-perfect match for Meridian: federal civilian benefits program, Snowflake/dbt/Airflow stack we've delivered multiple times, SSIS migration experience from three past engagements, contract value in our sweet spot ($3.8M), and strong past performance citations available (Maryland DHS benefits work, Commerce trade data, CMS healthcare pipelines). Small business set-aside we qualify for, Secret clearance we hold, and 61 days to respond with high reuse potential from similar past proposals.

open_questions.why_it_matters: Affects technical approach complexity and risk profile. Net-new implementation requires more foundational architecture work; expansion of existing environment requires integration with current patterns.

open_questions.why_it_matters: Affects pricing strategy and level of investment in technical approach. RFP Section 6 states 'Best-value evaluation' but doesn't specify tradeoff methodology or price sensitivity.

The Snowflake-specific federal healthcare experience requirement also reveals that 'past performance relevance' scoring could be more granular - we may want to separate 'domain match' from 'technology stack match' from 'scale match' as sub-scores rather than holistic dimension score. Overall, rubric performed well - the weighted dimensions captured the key decision factors and the 82 fit score feels calibrated to the strength of this opportunity.

dimensions.name: Technical capability match
dimensions.reasoning: Very strong technical capability match with most requirements in our core capabilities. RFP requires: (1) Snowflake data platform - we list 'Snowflake and Databricks delivery experience across 40+ projects' as core capability; (2) dbt transformation framework - we explicitly cite 'dbt' in our ETL/ELT core capability with 'Strong track record migrating SSIS and Informatica workloads to modern orchestration'; (3) Apache Airflow orchestration - listed in our core ETL/ELT capability; (4) Tableau dashboards with row-level security - listed as core analytics capability; (5) Data governance using Collibra or Alation - we list 'Collibra and Alation implementations' as a core data governance capability; (6) SSIS pipeline migration - explicitly called out in our ETL/ELT description. evidence_citations: RFP header: 'Township of Cherry Hill, Camden County, New Jersey'
Section 3.5: 'Vendor must provide the Township with on-site onboarding and training'
Company profile: 'primary: [DC metro, Maryland, Virginia], secondary: [Delaware, Pennsylvania, North Carolina]'

rationale.confidence_notes: RFP scope is clearly defined across 20+ pages of technical specifications. The work is unambiguously outside our stated domain (explicitly listed in 'explicitly_not' capabilities). No ambiguity in disqualification rationale.
rationale.effort_reasoning: Despite being out of scope, this RFP would require high effort to respond: 100+ pages of specifications, detailed cost breakdown by year and line item, multiple mandatory certifications (EEO, political contribution disclosure, ownership disclosure, non-collusion affidavit, debarment affidavit, Iran/Russia certifications), three references with detailed contact information, narrative demonstrating understanding of scope, example invoice, and technical approach for AI-powered inspection platform. Estimated 120-160 hours to produce compliant response even if we had relevant capabilities.
rationale.calibration_notes: This RFP is a clear example of domain mismatch that should be caught immediately by hard disqualifier hd_out_of_domain. The rubric performed as intended - out-of-domain trigger correctly identified the fundamental misalignment. Dimension scores are artificially low but still computed for calibration purposes as instructed. One observation: the 'contract_value_fit' dimension scored 40 despite being below sweet spot because it's technically above the hard floor - this might be worth revisiting in rubric to penalize more heavily when value is far below sweet spot even if above minimum. Also note: deadline_feasibility scored 0 due to passed deadline, but this wasn't a hard disqualifier in current rubric - consider adding 'hd_deadline_passed' as explicit disqualifier for future versions.
rationale.recommendation_rationale: This RFP is for road and asset management software with AI-powered pavement inspection and municipal asset tracking - fundamentally outside Meridian's data platform and analytics domain. The scope requires hardware provision (8 mobile devices with mounting equipment), computer vision AI for infrastructure inspection, and GIS-based asset management software - none of which align with our core capabilities in cloud data warehousing, ETL/ELT pipelines, or analytics enablement. This is a specialized municipal infrastructure software procurement, not a data modernization engagement.

hard_disqualifier_results.evidence: No security clearance requirements mentioned in RFP. This is a local government procurement for commercial software.
hard_disqualifier_results.reasoning: Municipal contract with no federal security requirements. Not applicable.

hard_disqualifier_results.evidence: RFP states '5-year term' with hardware (8 devices), software licensing for up to 15 users, training, and AI-powered inspection platform. Cost sheet indicates multi-year software licensing plus hardware. Estimated value likely $300K-$800K total over 5 years based on typical municipal SaaS pricing.
hard_disqualifier_results.reasoning: While exact value not disclosed, scope suggests contract above $250K threshold. However, this is borderline and should be verified - municipal road management software with AI capabilities for 242 centerline miles could range from $250K-$1M depending on vendor.

hard_disqualifier_results.evidence: RFP states 'fair and open process in accordance with N.J.S.A. 19:44A-20.5 et seq.' No set-aside designation mentioned.
hard_disqualifier_results.reasoning: Open competition procurement. No set-aside restrictions apply.

hard_disqualifier_results.evidence: Section 3 Scope of Work specifies: 'technology (including data collection hardware and software employing artificial intelligence) capable of performing rapid, objective condition assessments on pavement surfaces and other assets including but not limited to manholes, storm sewer inlets and signs.' Requirements include: mobile device-based image capture, AI-powered pavement damage classification (15+ damage types), roadway sign inventory management, stormwater inlet tracking, manhole tracking, GPS tracking module, and integration with ESRI ArcGIS and Spatial Data Logics. Section 3.2 requires provision of '8 electronic devices with mounting capabilities to a vehicle's windshield or dash' with 5-year warranty.
hard_disqualifier_results.reasoning: This is specialized municipal infrastructure inspection and asset management software - fundamentally outside Meridian's stated domain. Our company profile explicitly lists under 'explicitly_not': 'Custom application development outside of data domain' and 'Hardware procurement or integration.' This RFP requires both: purpose-built computer vision software for pavement inspection AND hardware provision (mobile devices with vehicle mounts). While there is a data component (asset tracking, reporting), the core deliverable is vertical-specific infrastructure management software, not a data platform or analytics solution.

similar_past_proposals_analysis.why_relevant: Federal data modernization with strong past performance narrative, but completely different domain (trade data vs. municipal infrastructure). Executive summary structure could be template.

Healthcare data pipeline work demonstrates our core capability (ETL/data engineering) but has no relevance to computer vision AI or asset management software. Past performance section structure could be reused.
